risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 (SAS Institute Inc, Cary, North Carolina, USA) to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence by a statistician, which is a proper random method.

Quote: "A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession... to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups." Comment: This describes sequence generation and blinding of the statistician/investigators, but does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism preventing the person enrolling participants from foreseeing assignments (e.g. central allocation, SNOSE, pharmacy-controlled assignment). As this was a cluster-randomised trial and the report does not state whether recruitment/enrolment occurred before or after knowledge of cluster assignment, allocation concealment is not clearly established.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The morbidity results reported in the current paper are from the iLiNS-Zinc study, a community-based, double-blind, placebo-controlled, cluster-randomised trial..." and "During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups." and "All SQ-LNS products had the same appearance, aroma and flavour, and the zinc and placebo dispersible tablets were identical in appearance and flavour." Comment: The report explicitly states that participants/caregivers and key study personnel/field staff were blinded throughout the trial, and describes matching placebo/active products that support successful masking. Because the main outcomes included caregiver-reported morbidity (e.g. diarrhoea, fever, respiratory symptoms), which could be influenced by knowledge of allocation, adequate blinding reduces performance bias risk.

Quote: "During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups." and "Field data collectors used a structured questionnaire to collect a weekly morbidity history, including the child's general state, appetite, number of semiliquid/liquid stools, presence of blood or mucus in stools, vomiting, fever, signs of respiratory tract infections..." Comment: Outcomes were assessed mainly by caregiver report collected by field data collectors, with some measured components (auricular temperature, RDT, blood smear). The assessors therefore included caregiver self-report and field staff. Because the trial explicitly states that both participants/caregivers and field staff were blinded, the subjective morbidity outcomes were assessed under blinded conditions, and the objective components were also unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. This supports low risk of detection bias.

Quote: "A total of 2435 children were enrolled in the study and 2364 of them were included in the final analyses after excluding participants with less than 30 days of morbidity observations." and "Caregivers of 97% of the participating children provided information on at least 30 days of morbidity surveillance... The 71 children who were excluded from the final analyses because they provided less than 30 days of morbidity observations did not differ at baseline from those included in the analysis (data not shown). There were no differences in participation or reporting rates among the four intervention groups." Comment: For the analysed morbidity outcomes over the 9-month follow-up, 71/2435 (2.9%) were excluded post-randomization, leaving 2364 analysed. Table 1 and Table 2 allow per-arm randomized vs analysed counts to be derived: 602→579, 613→593, 603→593, 617→599, corresponding to attrition of 3.8%, 3.3%, 1.7%, and 2.9%, respectively. This is very low overall and balanced across groups, with no reported differences in participation/reporting rates, so incomplete outcome data are unlikely to bias the outcome estimates.
